text: For me, the baseline is not 1960, but 2000, when personal computers became a commodity, followed closely by the arrival of the Internet. We had strings and hash tables by then, so they became part of the commodity toolkit. From then on, technodiversity went down. We had lots of processor architectures in the 1990s, but only x86 and ARM survived the transition to commodity tech. Among the architectures that disappeared, the <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Transputer|transputer> is probably the attempt closest to your ideas.

text: Yeah, I was quite interested in the transputer and OCCAM. I perceive there being a hangover from the 1960s that we haven't shook ... so I guess that I think that commoditization happened much earlier than 2000 (I blame "C")... <https://programmingsimplicity.substack.com/p/weve-always-done-it-this-way?r=1egdky>
